texts.full_text: way of solving the crisis is an Italian govt bail-out. But EU “rules” are “preventing” this. EU wants the retail investors in bank bonds to take a hit. With a friend like EU, who needs enemies. From NYT Dealbook: ITALY’S PLAN FOR BANKS IS DIVIDING EUROPE The Italian government needs to spend an estimated $45 billion to shore up its banks, which are burdened with bad loans. But fears that European authorities will bar the government from providing this support is adding to turbulence in the aftermath of Britain’s vote to leave the European Union,Peter Eavis reports in DealBook. And the situation could keep investors on edge through the summer. Italian bank shares have dropped steeply – an indication of a storm ahead. The stock price of Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, one of Italy’s most troubled banks, has plummeted. Founded in 1472, it is the world’s oldest bank and its current struggles are symbolic of the broader issues facing the Italian banking sector. The European Central Bank (ECB) and the European Commission have been pressuring Italy to adhere to the EU's state aid rules, which are designed to prevent governments from distorting competition by giving their own industries unfair advantages. These rules typically require that shareholders and junior bondholders, including many retail investors, take losses before any state aid can be provided. This process, known as a "bail-in," is intended to ensure that the financial burden is shared more equitably and to avoid using taxpayer money to rescue banks. However, in Italy, many of these junior bondholders are ordinary citizens who were sold these bonds as safe investments. The prospect of these individuals losing their savings has sparked widespread public anger and political resistance to the EU's demands. The Italian government argues that a bail-in could lead to a loss of public confidence in the banking system and potentially trigger a broader financial crisis. This situation highl

texts.full_text: 3, 2018 Sign in to favorite this Discuss Comment, Blog about Share this Email, Facebook, Twitter Dr. Desiree Cox, MD, PhD By Dr Desiree Cox, MD, PhD ‘Everybody is a Genius,’ said Albert Einstein. ‘But if you judge a fish by its ability to climb a tree, it will live its whole life believing that it is stupid.’ This week, millions of students all over the world will be returning to classes to receive an education that’s setting them up to spend their whole lives believing they are stupid. No. This isn't just about academic success or failure—it's also a matter of personal growth and self-esteem. Education should not only impart knowledge but inspire students, helping each individual discover who they truly are as individuals with unique talents that can be nurtured to the fullest. It is crucial for educators at all levels to recognize this diversity in their pupils' abilities and adapt teaching methods accordingly—something often overlooked when standardized testing becomes a priority. The current educational system tends towards one-size-fits-all, which may not cater well enough to students with different learning styles or interests outside of the traditional academic subjects. This can lead them into believing that they are less capable than their peers who excel in these areas and resultantly stifle many young minds from reaching full potential. The importance lies within recognizing every student's individual worth—each has a unique combination of talents, strengths, weaknesses, passions or interests which should be encouraged to flourish. This is where the role for educators becomes paramount; they must understand that teaching isn't just about imparting knowledge but also nurturing creativity and innovation. It starts with changing our perspective on intelligence from one-dimensional (book-smarts) towards a multi-faceted view, acknowledging various forms of intelligences such as emotional quotient or creative thinking. It's time to move beyond the 'one size fits all' approach in education that has been largely unsuccessful at recognizing and nurturing individual talents. texts.full_text: e, see [@lecun2015deep; @choromanska2015loss; @dauphin2014identifying]. Performance is typically measured in terms of two metrics: training performance and generalization performance. Here we focus on the training performance of neural networks for binary classification, and provide conditions under which the training error is zero at all local minima of appropriately chosen surrogate loss functions. Our conditions are roughly in the following form: the neurons have to be increasing and strictly convex, the neural network should either be single-layered or is multi-layered with a shortcut-like connection, and the surrogate loss function should be a smooth version of hinge loss. We also provide counterexamples to show that, when these conditions are relaxed, the result may not hold.' author: - | Shiyu Liang[ [^1] ]{}\ sliang26@illinois.edu - | Ruoyu Sun[ ]{}\ ruoyus@illinoi.uiuc.edu --- ### Introduction The landscape of the loss function in neural networks has been a subject of extensive study due to its implications on training and optimization. A common conjecture is that all local minima lead to similar performance, which can be attributed partly to their proximity or equivalence under certain conditions [@lecun2015deep; @choromanska2015loss]. This notion has been supported by empirical evidence in various contexts but lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation. In this paper, we focus on the training error of neural networks for binary classification and provide sufficient conditions ensuring that all local minima result in zero training errors when using specific surrogate loss functions. ##

texts.full_text: pulses of a specified and known voltage (i.e., voltage regulated output pulses). For example, with reference to FIG. 1, a neurostimulator may have multiple output sources 1a and multiple sinks 1b (only one current source 1a and one current sink 1b shown in FIG. 1) that are configured to supply/receive stimulating current to/from the electrodes Ex, Ey, and ultimately to/from tissue (represented by load 5 having a resistance R). The source 1a and sink 1b are sometimes respectively referred to as PDACs and NDACs, reflecting the fact that the source 1a is typically formed of P-type transistors, while the sink 1b is typically formed of N-type transistors. The use of transistors of these polarities is sensible given that the source 1a is biased to a high voltage (V+), where P-type transistors are most logical, while the sink 1b is biased to a low voltage (V−), where N-type transistors are most logical, as shown in FIG. 1. A suitable current generator is disclosed in U.S. Pat. No. 6,181,969 (“the '969 patent”), which is expressly incorporated herein by reference in its entirety. The output current source 1a and output current sink 1b respectively include current generators 2a, 2b each configured to generate a reference current Iref, and digital-to-analog converter (DAC) circuitry 3a, 3b configured for regulating/amplifying the reference current Iref provided by the current generators 2a, 2b, and delivering output current Iout to the load 5 (having a resistance R). Specifically, the relation between Iout and Iref is determined in accordance with input bits arriving on busses 4a, 4b, which respectively give the output current source 1a and output current sink 1b their digital-to-analog functionality. There is a range of On Screen menus, accessible by pressing various buttons on the front panel, but I didn't need to operate any of them to get a perfectly adequate picture. I suppose it's worth pointing out what's missing; It doesn't have a built-in USB hub, as hinted at before, the power switch doesn't actually disable the power brick, the speakers are 'passive' - ie no amplifier, and not all systems provide a sufficiently powerful audio output to satisfy most tastes. Saying that - you get pretty much everything you need. Normal 'windows' use is as good, if not better than most CRT pictures of equivalent size, contrast is 350:1, maximum video bandwidth is 135MHz, and the response time is a perfectly reasonable 14ms. DVD and analogue TV (from a card in the PC) is entirely watchable. Given that I got a £10 gift voucher with the unit (offer ends 6 November 2005) I felt that the £149 (less a tenner) I paid for this unit was as good value as you were likely to get anywhere! Efficacy of a Computerized Intervention on HIV and Intimate Partner Violence Among Substance-Using Women in Community Corrections: A Randomized Controlled Trial. doi:10.2105/AJPH.2016.303119 Masivukeni Masivukeni, which translates to ‘Let’s wake up!’ in the Xhosa language, is a computer-supported curriculum designed to help patients in South Africa stay on their antiretroviral medication. Masivukeni provides lay counselors with an intervention roadmap and scripts that help them communicate to their patients the importance of adherence to treatment plans. In addition, the Masivukeni tool features interactive activities to help patients build their support network, develop problem-solving skills, visualize their pill regimen, and learn how HIV affects the body as viral resistance develops. Masivukeni is a collaboration between Dr. Robert Remien, a research scientist at the HIV Center for Clinical and Behavioral Studies; researchers at the University of Cape Town; and CCNMTL. It was developed with assistance from counselors, nurses, and volunteers at the Hout Bay clinic outside of Cape Town, South Africa. The project is supported by a five-year, $3 million grant from the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) and builds on a 2007 NIMH grant that facilitated developing and piloting Masivukeni. Masivukeni is now being tested in a randomized control trial at two clinics outside Cape Town, South Africa. J. Rowe, A. Kingon, A. Pearson, C. Mellins, R. Robbins, H. Gouse, J. Joska, M. Henry, R. texts.full_text: Although most patients with acute pancreatitis can be managed with conservative, supportive, and non-operative treatment, in selected cases, acute recurrent pancreatitis can be a challenging and recurrent condition, with patients experiencing recurrent episodes of acute pancreatitis, often with severe and debilitating pain, and in some cases, severe complications, such as pancreatic pseudocyst, pancreatic necrosis, and even pancreatic cancer. In such cases, a sphincteroplasty, a surgical procedure, may be considered as a potential treatment option, with the aim of improving pancreatic drainage and reducing the frequency and severity of pancreatic attacks. Sphincteroplasty, as a treatment option, may be considered in the following cases of acute recurrent pancreatitis: 1. Persistent pancreatic ductal obstruction: In cases of acute recurrent pancreatitis, the pancreatic duct may be obstructed, leading to a buildup of pancreatic enzymes, which in turn, may be a contributing factor to the recurrent pancreatic attacks. In such cases, a sphincteroplasty may be considered to improve pancreatic ductal drainage, thereby, reducing the recurrent attacks of pancreatitis. 2. Severe pancreatic ductal stones: In cases of recurrent pancreatitis, the pancreatic duct may be filled with stones, which may be a recurrent problem. Specific activities for thioredoxin reductase and glutathione reductase substrates for both TGRs explored were in the range or lower than values obtained for other platyhelminths and mammalian TGRs. cTsTGR and mTsTGR also showed reductase activity using hydroperoxide as substrate. Km(DTNB) and Kcat(DTNB) valueKm(DTNB) values represent the Michaelis-Menten constant for the thioredoxin reductase (TR) substrate, while kcat(DTNB) represents the turnover number. These parameters are crucial in understanding enzyme efficiency and affinity towards its substrates. The cTsTGR exhibited a higher Km value compared to mTsTGR, indicating that it has lower affinity for the thioredoxin reductase substrate at physiological conditions. However, this difference was not significant when comparing their kcat values, suggesting both enzymes have similar efficiency in reducing glutathione disulfide (GSSG) and oxidized nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) to its reduced forms under optimal cOnditions. To further elucidate the role of these TGRs, we performed kinetic analyses at different pH levels and temperatures. The cTsTGR demonstrated an optimum activity around neutral pH with a bell-shaped curve for temperature dependence, indicating that it is adapted to function in environments where cytosolic redox balance plays a crucial role. On the other hand, mTsTGR showed optimal activity under acidic conditions and lower thermal stability compared to its cytosolic counterpart, suggesting an adaptation suited towards mitochondrial compartments within parasitic tissues or cells undergoing stress at these pH levels. texts.full_text: t album from Laura Jane Grace & the Devouring Mothers, came into existence. Bought to Rot was written largely in motion—on tour, in Spain, Australia, Amsterdam hotel rooms, and some at home in Chicago. It’s a record scorched with honesty, unapologetically confessional, capturing many moments snipped from Grace’s life and stitched together in song. Although it’s a step and a twist away from Against Me!’s sonic blueprint, there’s still a kinetic punk energy that vibrates throughout. These compositions are looser, stripped, but with a melodic pop immediacy pushing to the fore. “I have my main gig, but I’m still doing this thing,” she continues. “It’s undeniable and it’s really good and here’s the proof … so what are you going to do with that?” Well, it has to go out into the world: via Bloodshot Records, the storied Chicago indie boasting a past & present roster that includes Ryan Adams, Neko Case, Murder by Death, Old 97’s and Justin Townes Ear

texts.full_text: nu Home About Contact Be Careful Not to Get Caught in The Rental Trap! There are many benefits to home ownership in Citrus County. One of the top benefits is being able to protect yourself from rising rents by locking in your housing cost for the life of your mortgage. Don’t Become Trapped A recent article by ConsumerAffairs addressed the continuous rise in rents, stating: “The cost of putting a roof over your head continues to go up. Not only are home prices still rising, but the cost of rent rose 0.5% in June.” Additionally, in the Joint Center for Housing Studies at Harvard University’s 2017 State of the Nation’s Housing Report, it was revealed that, “Despite a slight improvement ffrom the previous year, nearly half (48 percent) of renters are still cost burdened, meaning they spend more than 30 percent of their income on housing.” It's important to understand that when you're a renter, your landlord can increase your rent at any time. This can lead to financial instability and the constant worry of having to find a new place to live if you can't afford the rent hike. Homeownership provides a stable housing cost, especially with a fixed-rate mortgage where your principal and interest payments remain the same throughout the life of the loan. This stability can help you plan your budget and save for other important expenses or goals. If you're tired of throwing away money on rent each month and want to explore the benefits of homeownership, contact us today. We can help you find the perfect home in Citrus County that fits your budget and lifestyle. Don't get caught in the rental trap – take control of your housing situation by becoming a homeowner!

texts.full_text: species was first described by Edward Meyrick in 1883 under the name Larentia chlorias. Meyrick went on to give a more detailed description of the species in 1884. In 1903 George Hudson, thinking he was describing the species for the first time, also named the species Venusia princeps. In 1905 Meyrick synonymised this name and placed this species within the genus Xanthorhoe. In 1928 Hudson illustrated and discussed this species in his book "The Butterflies and Moths of New Zealand". The species was later transferred to its current placement by Ephraim Macropertagon Izzard, a British entomologist who specialized on the Geometridae family. In his work published between 1975-1986 he reclassified many moth families including this one renaming it Orthoclydon chlorias which is currently accepted as valid name for this species of moth in New Zealan
